text: have thought they were bots, but no . They all had belly buttons. Crazy as it seemed, they were human . She set it aside . The book at the bottom of the stack was different from the others. It wasn’t as wide, but it was thicker, the outside covered in old leather. She opened it and carefully looked through the pages . This one had been written by hand. Somehow, that made it more special. She’d never met anyone who could read, much less write, so it seemed an impossible skill. Though she couldn’t understand the words, for a moment she felt closer to the past than ever before. In the center, she found a few loose photos. The same people were present through all of them — a man, a woman, and two children. Had they lived in this house? Hidden in this room ? She glanced at the blankets and clothing, the single chair, the jars on the shelf. It had likely only been one person here . Replacing the pictures, she went to the table and gently laid the book atop it. Her eyes fell on the stacked boxes. What other little treasures were here to discover ? * * * R onin entered his dwelling, closing and locking the door behind him. His fingertips lingered on the knob. It was cold and smooth; texture and accurate temperature readings added dimensions to touch that he’d forgotten about during his long time in the Dust . The sun sank toward the western horizon, streaming orange light through the front windows which cast long shadows over his worktable. He’d hoped Lara would be in the main room when he returned, sitting at the table or perched on one of the wide window sills, but he doubted she could sit still for very long under most circumstances . Crossing the room, he placed his pack on the table and pulled off his coat, running his fingers over the rough fabric before tossing it over the back of the chair. The needle and thread he’d used to repair her boot were still out; he’d have to put them away later . He stepped into the kitchen. She wasn’t there, either, and why would she be? There wasn’t any food for her yet. He grasped the refrigerator by the sides, pulled it away from the wall, and slipped behind it to plug the cord into the electrical outlet . Something inside it rattled for several seconds before the sound evened to a soft, steady hum. He was, for once, grateful for the maintenance bots keeping the home appliances in Cheyenne in working order. Lara’s food would last longer in the refrigerator, which meant fewer trips to the vendor for Ronin . He pressed his hand to the refrigerator’s flat face, the sensors in his skin detecting the minute grooves in the brushed metal, and pushed it back into place. He’d store the newly-obtained food inside soon enough. Lara was likely hungry after his five-hour absence, and she’d appreciate choosing what food to eat first . Moving to the foot of the stairs, he called her name . Seconds ticked by. After a full minute had passed, he went up. She was likely resting. Ronin understood sleep only in that it was a necessary biological function for humans, much like her having to go . He could never truly experience it, so he didn’t truly comprehend, but he would still accommodate her needs . All the upstairs doors were open. Though there wasn’t much to see, she’d been familiarizing herself with her surroundings. Many of the home’s furnishings had been removed before Ronin took up residence here, and even the few remaining pieces were more than he required . He went to her room, finding the bed made — inexpertly — and her trinkets spread atop the dresser. Otherwise, there was no sign of her . The deadbolt had been locked upon his return, and he’d detected no errant air currents indicating an open window. Could she have been taken? Or had she found a way out on her own ? He focused his optics on her collection, and, after moving further into her room, discovered her boots and bag on the floor beside the dresser. No, she hadn’t left on her own. She’d never leave these things behind . She wasn’t in the other bedrooms, and his gear — including the pistol — was still where he left it, atop the chest at the foot of his bed. More evidence that she hadn’t departed on her own. All these items would aid in her survival; to leave behind her own belongings and his openly displayed tools was madness . His processors whirred as he returned to the hallway and paced from one end to the other, running hundreds, thousands of simulations at once. None of them could fully account for the single greatest variable — human unpredictability . Taken, then? He was certain Warlord kept keys to every residence in the Bot District, and it would only require a single gearhead to subdue Lara. What history did she have with Cheyenne’s ruler ? Ronin halted abruptly, tilting his head down. Thick clumps of dust lay on the carpet, some already trampled into the fibers by his boots. His optics swept up to the attic hatch . He’d hadn’t gone up there once since taking up residence. Why would she ? Reaching up, he grasped the broken pull-string and tugged the hatch open . The whine of old springs and hinges warned him. He raised his free hand and caught the ladder before it connected with his head. Above, someone gasped . Floorboards creaked overhead, and Lara peered over the edge, with cobwebs clinging to her messy hair and a smudge of dirt on one cheek. Briefly, he shut out everything but his sensory input. Lara was here . Safe . And she was beautiful . “Did you know somebody lived up
